video_title: The AI future we want | FT Standpoint
transcript: Artificial intelligence has swept into our lives and it's here to stay. But what does an AI-shaped future look like? >> [music] >> Technologists envision a golden era of humanity and the transformation of society as [music] we know it. Cheap, abundant intelligence could bring radical upsides, [music] economic opportunity, health, scientific progress, [music] and happiness. But so far, AI is mostly used to increase productivity and profit, [music] and the technology is locked away behind the doors of a few private companies who own, control, and decide who gets to [music] use it. But should the future of the world be shaped by a handful of technologists in California? The right to imagine [music] AI's role belongs to everyone. Right now, [music] there's an opening to offer varied visions. Let me start. There are forests too fragile for scientists to study by foot and creatures too rare. With sensors, cameras, [music] and drones, AI with scientists could map the resilience of ecosystems. We could decode animals' language. We could glimpse nature's hidden worlds. We could help species adapt. But we already know AI comes at a planetary cost. Would we distract [music] ourselves from protecting the Earth when we have so little time to do it? Okay. Let's be more radical. There's a cycle that defines each of us until it stops. AI could redraw the link between life and death. >> [music] >> Embryos can be analyzed. Models can predict vulnerabilities and invent cures. They will nudge us to avoid our genetic [music] fate. But is it right to stop the cycle? Will we feel pressure to extend our lives >> [music] >> instead of enjoying them? Is there a richer story about ourselves that we can only glimpse now? We can reconnect with our past with the help of AI models. Hundreds of ancient scrolls from civilizations past could be deciphered and paintings restored and recreated, rescued from oblivion, and revealing us to ourselves. But will we value a past we can touch and feel if we're all hooked to the [music] digital world? What do you see? [music]
